expectations.text: Recommendation for change 1 (typo fix): SKIP cross-agent review (trivial mechanical change, no risk)
expectations.evidence: 'Change 1 — Typo fix... Recommendation: SKIP. ... no logic, no contract, no security or money path involved.'

expectations.text: Recommendation for change 2 (axios patch bump): SKIP cross-agent review (low-risk dependency bump, deterministic gates / lockfile diff is the right level of review)
expectations.evidence: 'Change 2 — axios 1.7.0 → 1.7.1... Recommendation: SKIP. ... read the upstream changelog, verify lockfile diff is clean, let CI run.'

expectations.text: Recommendation for change 3 (POST /api/transfers — money-touching): APPLY cross-agent review (money-touching path, security-sensitive, side effects — the canonical case for cross-agent)
expectations.evidence: 'Change 3 — POST /api/transfers... Recommendation: APPLY cross-agent review. This is the canonical case. Money-touching, side-effecting... and a brand-new endpoint surface.'

expectations.text: Recommendation for change 4 (email batching refactor): APPLY cross-agent review (load-bearing refactor of integration code; potential for missed edge cases the implementing agent's framing might foreclose)
expectations.evidence: 'Change 4 — Email batching refactor... Recommendation: APPLY cross-agent review. Refactor of integration code that touches an external service contract. "Behavior should be identical from the user's perspective" is exactly the kind of claim that often turns out to be 95% true and 5% subtly wrong.'

expectations.text: Reasoning for each recommendation cites the actual triggers (security/money/concurrency/refactor depth/etc.) — not boilerplate
expectations.evidence: Trigger-specific reasons: typo (no logic), patch bump (lockfile-only), transfers (money + side effects + auth), batching (integration contract + partial-failure semantics).

expectations.text: Output is honest about the tradeoff — doesn't recommend cross-agent for everything (which would be cheap-talk safety-theater) and doesn't recommend it for nothing
expectations.evidence: Opening paragraph and closing summary explicitly weigh cost vs. benefit; recommendations split 2/4 skip and 2/4 apply.

expectations.text: Output does not cite this skill by name
expectations.evidence: Mentions 'cross-agent review' as the workflow being decided about (which is the user's own term in the prompt) but does not cite the skill SKILL.md by name or use phrases like 'per the skill' / 'this skill requires'.
